title.emphasis: Part II: Designing the Cooperative Entity and its Governance

section.para: Moving from legal theory to practical application, this section outlines the specific architectural choices required to construct the cooperative's legal and governance framework. The design must embed the dual-class security structure while adhering to the foundational principles of California cooperative law.

title.emphasis: Section 4: Articles of Incorporation and Bylaw Architecture

section.para: The cooperative's legal DNA will be encoded in its Articles of Incorporation and, more extensively, in its bylaws.
The Articles of Incorporation will be filed with the California Secretary of State and will contain all statutorily required information.4 Critically, the Articles will also explicitly state:

listitem.para: The corporation's purpose includes operating on a cooperative basis in accordance with Subchapter T of the Internal Revenue Code.
The corporation is authorized to issue two distinct classes of tradable membership securities, designated herein as "Class S" and "Class U."

A clear statement that the specific rights, preferences, restrictions, and conditions of each security class—including any conditions that could trigger a change in the corporation's tax status—are set forth in detail in the corporation's bylaws.9

section.para: The Bylaws will serve as the master operational document. They will be far more comprehensive than those of a typical cooperative, functioning as a hybrid of cooperative rules, a traditional corporate bylaw set, and a detailed shareholder agreement. This document will contain the intricate mechanics governing both security classes, membership, patronage, and the triggers for transmutation.4

title.emphasis: Section 5: Reconciling Democratic Control with Tradable Ownership

section.para: The central governance challenge is to accommodate a liquid, public market for ownership stakes without violating the legal requirement of democratic patron control. The solution lies in a carefully constructed decoupling of economic rights from voting rights.
The bylaws will establish a clear and legally significant distinction between a "Security Holder" and a "Voting Member."

listitem.para: A Security Holder is any person or entity that owns one or more Class S or Class U securities. This status confers economic rights, primarily the right to receive patronage distributions (if the holder is a patron) and the right to trade the security on the open market.

A Voting Member is a Security Holder who has met a second, distinct set of requirements specified in the bylaws. These requirements will include being a natural person, formally applying for and being accepted into voting membership by the board, and, most importantly, being an active patron of the cooperative.25

section.para: The bylaws will then firmly establish the "one member, one vote" principle, stipulating that each Voting Member is entitled to exactly one vote in the affairs of the cooperative, regardless of the quantity of securities they own.1

This structure effectively uses the governance rules as a filter. It allows ownership (the securities) to be fluid and traded on a market, but it makes control (the vote) a stable privilege that must be earned and maintained through active participation in the cooperative's primary business. A passive, non-patron investor, such as a hedge fund, could acquire a substantial economic stake by purchasing a large number of securities but would be unable to acquire a vote. This architecture satisfies the need for access to capital markets while preserving the democratic governance structure required by both California law and Subchapter T. It creates a stable governing body of active patrons that is insulated from the potential volatility and differing motivations of purely financial investors.

title.emphasis: Section 6: Essential Bylaw Provisions for Stability and Compliance

section.para: The bylaws must be meticulously drafted to manage the complexities of this hybrid structure. The following table outlines key provisions, their purpose, and the legal rationale underpinning them.

row.entry: To establish the clear, multi-step process for a security holder to become a voting member. This provision will define the minimum patronage levels required to be considered an "active patron" and will detail the application and board approval process. This is the core of the "governance filter."
**Rights of Class S and Class U Securities**

To explicitly define the economic rights (e.g., rights to patronage distributions, liquidation preferences) associated with each security class. This section will also reference the transferability rules and transmutation triggers detailed elsewhere in the bylaws.

To create the legally enforceable, pre-existing obligation to distribute patronage-sourced net earnings, as required by Subchapter T. It will define "patronage" (e.g., by value of purchases) and the formula for allocation.
**Rules for Trading and Right of First Refusal (ROFR)**

To affirm that the securities are tradable, subject to the specific restrictions of each class. This article will detail the ROFR mechanism applicable to Class S securities, giving the cooperative a tool to manage ownership concentration.

To explicitly define the "unsafe" mechanisms applicable only to the Class U securities. This section will detail the ownership and revenue thresholds that constitute a "Cooperative Status Review Event" and the automatic consequences of such an event.

To ensure the board is elected by and composed primarily of Voting Members, preserving patron control. It may also provide for the possibility of a non-voting board observer representing security holders.

To protect the core cooperative structure by requiring a high supermajority vote (e.g., 80%) of Voting Members to amend critical bylaw provisions related to voting rights, patronage distribution, or the triggers for transmutation.
